title: Estimating Hydrogeological Parameters at Groundwater Level Observation Wells Without Pumping Well Information

research.question: Can representative storativity and pumping rate be estimated from observation-well drawdown when pumping-well number, locations, and rates are unknown?
research.system: A confined aquifer observed at one monitoring well amid multiple pumping wells distributed within a finite influence circle.
research.inputData: Synthetic intermittent-pumping drawdown series generated with the Theis solution under varying well distances, arrangements, and transmissivity anisotropy.
research.method: Average the Theis impulse response over a circular pumping domain to obtain an equivalent impulse response, infer equivalent pumping from drawdown, and fit storativity, equivalent rate, and influence radius with constrained nonlinear optimization.
research.benchmark: Exact multiwell Theis superposition synthetic data, distance and anisotropy experiments, residual metrics, and Morris sensitivity analysis.
research.uncertainty: Measurement-error weighting enters the objective; Morris elementary effects assess sensitivity and nonlinear interaction; synthetic experiments expose bias from distance, arrangement, and anisotropy; known transmissivity is required to avoid nonunique T-S solutions.
research.conclusion: Under dense, approximately uniform pumping and known transmissivity, the equivalent response can recover representative storativity more robustly than equivalent pumping rate from an observation-well hydrograph.

narrative.throughline: Many unseen pumping wells can be replaced by one equivalent circular response only when the averaging geometry and identifiability limits remain visible.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience knows the Theis drawdown response for a known pumping well and observation distance.
narrative.stakes: Without a defensible spatial surrogate, abundant monitoring hydrographs cannot yield representative storage parameters and may be misread as single-well tests.
narrative.resolution: The equivalent response returns to synthetic observation wells with a clear distinction between stable storativity recovery and geometry-sensitive pumping estimates.

background.premise: Traditional pumping-test interpretation requires pumping-well number distance and rate information that observation networks often lack.
background.visibleEvidence: A monitored drawdown curve remains while source-well metadata disappear.
background.whyNow: This missing geometry is the paper's inverse problem.
background.boundary: The target is representative parameters near an observation well rather than reconstruction of every source well.
background.sourceExcerpt: Traditional modeling methods are not well suited for analyzing such data because they lack essential details about the pumping wells
background.visualObject: Observation hydrograph with unknown pumping sources
background.motionPurpose: Remove source labels while preserving the combined drawdown.
background.stepDetail: Show multiple wells; display rates and distances; hide those details; retain the observation trace.
background.transitionBridge: The missing wells become the finite averaging circle in M02.

background.premise: Theis impulse responses depend on relative pumping-observation positions.
background.visibleEvidence: The same pumping pulse produces different drawdown kernels as source distance changes.
background.whyNow: Spatial averaging must begin from the distance-dependent single-well kernel.
background.boundary: The governing kernel is for confined flow under Theis assumptions.
background.sourceExcerpt: knowledge of the relative spatial positions between pumping and ob-
background.visualObject: Single-well Theis kernels indexed by radial distance
background.motionPurpose: Move a pumping well radially and reshape the impulse response.
background.stepDetail: Fix the observation well; move the source; show the distance; reveal the changing kernel.
background.transitionBridge: The distance-indexed kernels become inputs to the spatial integration in M02.

background.premise: The paper identifies high pumping-well density as a setting where the equivalent response is particularly useful.
background.visibleEvidence: A sparse point field becomes a high-density pumping field inside the influence circle.
background.whyNow: The stated application setting bounds the spatial surrogate before its construction.
background.boundary: High density supports use of the surrogate but does not establish uniform well placement or unbiased recovery.
background.sourceExcerpt: density of agricultural pumping wells can exceed 210 well/km2
background.visualObject: High-density pumping field within an influence circle
background.motionPurpose: Increase the visible well count without implying perfectly uniform placement.
background.stepDetail: Show sparse wells; increase the point density; retain irregular locations inside the circle.
background.transitionBridge: The high-density setting becomes the finite averaging domain in M02.

methods.input: External pumping stresses and a single-well Theis impulse response
methods.output: Time-varying confined-aquifer drawdown
methods.validity: Linear TFN superposition represents multiple time-dependent pumping stresses.
methods.sourceExcerpt: TFN modeling characterizes the response of groundwater drawdown, denoted as
methods.visualObject: Pumping pulses sliding through a Theis kernel into a drawdown hydrograph
methods.motionPurpose: Show how each pumping event contributes to the observed trace.
methods.stepDetail: Place pulses; align the kernel; scale each response; sum the overlapping curves.
methods.transitionBridge: The single-source responses become spatial inputs to M02.

methods.input: Distance-dependent Theis impulse responses over a finite pumping circle
methods.output: Equivalent impulse response function for spatially homogeneous pumping
methods.validity: Equation 4 averages the distance-dependent impulse response over the finite circular domain.
methods.sourceExcerpt: assuming spatial homogeneity in
methods.visualObject: Concentric rings of Theis kernels collapsing into one impulse kernel
methods.motionPurpose: Average radial contributions while keeping the finite influence radius visible.
methods.stepDetail: Partition the circle into rings and integrate the weighted impulse kernels.
methods.transitionBridge: Integrate the impulse kernel through time to obtain the unit response in M03.

methods.input: Equivalent impulse response function over elapsed time
methods.output: Equivalent unit response function for the finite pumping circle
methods.validity: Equations 5 and 6 define the unit response from the equivalent impulse response before pumping is inferred.
methods.sourceExcerpt: is called an equivalent IRF
methods.visualObject: Equivalent impulse kernel accumulating into a unit-step response
methods.motionPurpose: Separate the instantaneous kernel from its cumulative unit response.
methods.stepDetail: Sweep across elapsed time and accumulate the impulse area into the unit-response curve.
methods.transitionBridge: Use the unit response to infer equivalent pumping in M04.

methods.input: Observed drawdown changes and the equivalent unit response
methods.output: Equivalent pumping-rate time series around the observation well
methods.validity: The approach treats short drawdown intervals as pumping dominated and assumes limited competing external forcing.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2, Equations (6)-(7); PDF page 7, Discussion, opening paragraphs
methods.sourceExcerpt: To retrieve pumping activities from drawdown observations, let the
methods.visualObject: Observed drawdown steps transformed into equivalent pumping bars
methods.motionPurpose: Reverse the response relation so head changes become pumping estimates.
methods.stepDetail: Divide each drawdown increment by its equivalent unit response and place the inferred pumping bar.
methods.transitionBridge: Calculate residuals between modeled and measured drawdown in M05.

methods.input: Measured drawdown and equivalent-response predictions for trial parameters
methods.output: Least-squares residual objective over the parameter space
methods.validity: Equation 8 defines the objective from squared differences at the drawdown measurement times.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the number of drawdown measurements
methods.visualObject: Measured points dropping vertical residuals to a trial response curve
methods.motionPurpose: Convert prediction mismatch into the objective used by the inversion.
methods.stepDetail: Draw one residual at each measurement and accumulate the squared lengths.
methods.transitionBridge: Transform positive unknowns before searching the residual surface in M06.

methods.input: Positive transmissivity storativity pumping-rate and radius parameters
methods.output: Logarithmic parameter coordinates for optimization
methods.validity: The source performs optimization on logarithmic values because all unknown parameters are positive.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3, paragraph after Equation (8)
methods.sourceExcerpt: parameter optimization is carried out on the logarithmic scale of these values
methods.visualObject: Positive parameter axes relabeled as logarithmic coordinates
methods.motionPurpose: Keep the search in the positive physical domain.
methods.stepDetail: Map each positive parameter marker to its logarithmic coordinate.
methods.transitionBridge: Apply the transmissivity constraint to the transformed search in M07.

methods.input: Logarithmic parameter search with correlated transmissivity and storativity
methods.output: Constrained parameter space with reduced T-S nonuniqueness
methods.validity: The source states that additional transmissivity constraints can enforce solution uniqueness when T and S are both unknown.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3, paragraph after Equation (8)
methods.sourceExcerpt: To ensure solution uniqueness, additional constraints on
methods.visualObject: Broad T-S residual valley clipped by a transmissivity band
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the nonunique direction and bound it with known transmissivity information.
methods.stepDetail: Overlay the target transmissivity band on the logarithmic residual surface.
methods.transitionBridge: Search the constrained residual surface with differential evolution in M08.

methods.input: Constrained logarithmic residual objective
methods.output: Population of candidate parameter sets near low residual values
methods.validity: The study couples least squares with differential evolution for the nonlinear parameter search.
methods.sourceExcerpt: least squares method, coupled with the differential evolution algo-
methods.visualObject: Candidate population moving across the constrained residual surface
methods.motionPurpose: Show the global search approaching low-residual regions.
methods.stepDetail: Move candidate markers across the permitted surface according to differential evolution.
methods.transitionBridge: Identify the selected residual minimum in M09.

methods.input: Residual values from the constrained differential-evolution search
methods.output: Selected parameter set at the least-squares minimum
methods.validity: A selected minimum is interpretable only inside the imposed transmissivity constraint because the unconstrained residual map may lack a unique bull's-eye.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The residual map may show that the smallest residuals
methods.visualObject: Lowest residual marker selected from the constrained search surface
methods.motionPurpose: Separate the final selected parameter set from the search population.
methods.stepDetail: Mark the lowest permitted residual and retain its parameter coordinates.
methods.transitionBridge: Generate controlled drawdown observations for recovery tests in M10.

methods.input: Known intermittent pumping histories and multiwell Theis responses
methods.output: Synthetic intermittent-pumping drawdown observations
methods.validity: The superposition technique of the Theis solution supplies drawdown data with known generating conditions.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3, paragraph after Equation (9)
methods.sourceExcerpt: technique of Theis solution to produce synthetic drawdown data
methods.visualObject: Known pumping bars producing exact multiwell drawdown points
methods.motionPurpose: Create a controlled observation series whose generating parameters remain visible.
methods.stepDetail: Apply Theis superposition to the known intermittent pumping history.
methods.transitionBridge: Fit the equivalent response to each synthetic case in M11.

methods.input: Synthetic drawdown observations with constrained transmissivity
methods.output: Estimated storativity pumping rate and influence radius for each case
methods.validity: Positive estimates are obtained in logarithmic space with transmissivity constrained to its target range.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 3.1.1, paragraph before Table 2
methods.sourceExcerpt: The proposed function fits the drawdown data for each pumping phase
methods.visualObject: Equivalent-response curve settling onto synthetic drawdown points
methods.motionPurpose: Constrain one equivalent-response parameter set against each synthetic case.
methods.stepDetail: Fit the equivalent response to one synthetic drawdown case.
methods.transitionBridge: Calculate standard error of estimate from the fitted case in M12.

methods.input: Synthetic drawdown observations and fitted predictions
methods.output: Standard error of estimate for the fitted drawdown
methods.validity: Equation 10a scales squared prediction discrepancies by the stated degrees of freedom.
methods.sourceExcerpt: used the standard error of the estimate (SEE)
methods.visualObject: Squared drawdown discrepancies feeding an SEE gauge
methods.motionPurpose: Reduce fit dispersion to the paper's standard-error measure.
methods.stepDetail: Square the fitted discrepancies and scale their sum by the degrees of freedom.
methods.transitionBridge: Calculate mean error from the signed discrepancies in M13.

methods.input: Signed differences between synthetic observations and fitted predictions
methods.output: Mean error for the fitted drawdown
methods.validity: Equation 10b averages signed prediction errors to retain directional bias.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the mean error (ME) to assess prediction errors
methods.visualObject: Signed drawdown discrepancies balancing on an ME gauge
methods.motionPurpose: Expose systematic overprediction or underprediction separately from SEE.
methods.stepDetail: Average the signed fitted discrepancies into the ME value.
methods.transitionBridge: Index both error measures by source distance in M14.

methods.input: Synthetic pumping cases at five observation distances
methods.output: Distance-indexed parameter estimates and fit errors
methods.validity: The experiment uses observation distances of 200 500 1000 1500 and 2000 m under the same intermittent pumping schedule.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 3.1.1, Figure 2 and Table 2
methods.sourceExcerpt: five observation well distances from the pumping well: 200 m, 500 m
methods.visualObject: One pumping well stepping through five radii around the observation well
methods.motionPurpose: Expose how increasing source distance changes equivalent parameters and fit error.
methods.stepDetail: Evaluate the fitted recovery at each stated distance.
methods.transitionBridge: Replace one source with three pumping arrangements in M15.

methods.input: Three multiwell distance arrangements with equal pumping rates
methods.output: Arrangement-indexed parameter estimates and drawdown fits
methods.validity: The scenarios preserve a target total pumping rate of 300 m3 per hour while changing well distances.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 3.1.2, Figure 3 and Table 3
methods.sourceExcerpt: The effectiveness of the proposed function is evaluated by analyzing
methods.visualObject: Three multiwell configurations surrounding the same observation well
methods.motionPurpose: Isolate the effect of source arrangement on equivalent recovery.
methods.stepDetail: Evaluate the fitted recovery for each stated three-well arrangement.
methods.transitionBridge: Change transmissivity anisotropy in M16.

methods.input: Synthetic drawdown under five transmissivity anisotropy ratios
methods.output: Anisotropy-indexed drawdown maps parameter estimates and fit errors
methods.validity: Equation 14 generates ratios 0.1 0.5 1 5 and 10 with well and baseline parameters fixed.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 3.1.3, Figure 4 and Table 4
methods.sourceExcerpt: The effects of anisotropy on the
methods.visualObject: Five anisotropic drawdown maps sharing one well location
methods.motionPurpose: Reveal how directional transmissivity changes drawdown and recovered parameters.
methods.stepDetail: Evaluate the fitted recovery at each stated anisotropy ratio.
methods.transitionBridge: Sample Morris trajectories over the response parameters in M17.

methods.input: Equivalent-response model with parameter ranges and a 24-hour evaluation time
methods.output: One-at-a-time elementary effects across sampled parameter values
methods.validity: The Morris workflow disturbs sampled parameter values one at a time over the stated ranges.
methods.sourceExcerpt: uses sampling at a time to disturb parameter values
methods.visualObject: One-at-a-time paths crossing a parameter grid
methods.motionPurpose: Make the sensitivity workflow distinct from its summary statistics.
methods.stepDetail: Sample a parameter point and move one coordinate to obtain each elementary effect.
methods.transitionBridge: Aggregate the elementary effects into Morris statistics in M18.

methods.input: Elementary effects from the Morris trajectories
methods.output: Overall-effect and nonlinear-interaction measures for each parameter
methods.validity: Equations 12a and 12b define the overall magnitude and spread of the elementary effects.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Two statistical measures
methods.visualObject: Elementary-effect clouds collapsing into paired Morris measures
methods.motionPurpose: Separate the effect computation from the preceding sampling workflow.
methods.stepDetail: Aggregate effect magnitudes and their spread for each parameter.
methods.transitionBridge: Use the bounded interpretation checks beginning with record isolation in M19.

methods.input: Observation-well drawdown record containing possible nonpumping forcings
methods.output: Short drawdown interval screened for precipitation and large irrigation influences
methods.validity: The discussion recommends a few days of data with no precipitation or large-scale local irrigation during use.
methods.sourceExcerpt: relatively short drawdown data
methods.visualObject: Long hydrograph clipped to a short pumping-dominated window
methods.motionPurpose: Keep only the period supported by the paper's forcing-isolation condition.
methods.stepDetail: Clip the record and screen the retained window for stated external forcings.
methods.transitionBridge: Evaluate whether pumping-well density matches the intended setting in M20.

methods.input: Pumping wells distributed around the observation well
methods.output: Density check for the intended high-density application setting
methods.validity: The paper states that the equivalent response is particularly useful where pumping-well density is high.
methods.sourceExcerpt: settings where the density of pumping wells around the observation
methods.visualObject: Well-count density gauge beside the influence circle
methods.motionPurpose: Treat high density as an application condition without equating it with uniform placement.
methods.stepDetail: Count visible sources per area and compare the field with the high-density setting.
methods.transitionBridge: Evaluate source-distance bias inside the influence circle in M21.

methods.input: Pumping wells at different radii inside the estimated influence circle
methods.output: Distance condition for representative storativity and pumping estimates
methods.validity: The discussion states that wells too close or too far from the observation well can understate or overstate storativity.
methods.sourceExcerpt: located too close or too far away from the observation well
methods.visualObject: Near and far source clusters deforming the equivalent circle
methods.motionPurpose: Show distance as a distinct spatial-condition check.
methods.stepDetail: Move sources inward and outward while tracking the direction of recovery bias.
methods.transitionBridge: Evaluate directional transmissivity bias in M22.

methods.input: Aquifer cases with different transmissivity anisotropy ratios
methods.output: Anisotropy condition on equivalent pumping-rate recovery
methods.validity: The paper reports that aquifer anisotropy can reduce pumping-rate sensitivity and bias estimation.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the anisotropy ratio of an aquifer can reduce the
methods.visualObject: Circular influence field stretching into directional ellipses
methods.motionPurpose: Keep anisotropy separate from source-distance effects.
methods.stepDetail: Stretch the transmissivity field and track the pumping-rate sensitivity change.
methods.transitionBridge: Apply the final known-transmissivity requirement in M23.

methods.input: Parameter inversion with correlated transmissivity and storativity
methods.output: Interpretation limited to known or independently constrained transmissivity
methods.validity: The paper identifies knowledge of transmissivity as the primary limitation for estimation accuracy and solution uniqueness.
methods.sourceExcerpt: method is that the estimation accuracy heavily depends on knowledge
methods.visualObject: Transmissivity value locking the final inversion boundary
methods.motionPurpose: Close the interpretation with the source's principal identifiability requirement.
methods.stepDetail: Lock transmissivity to independent information and retain only the bounded recovery claim.
methods.transitionBridge: Return the bounded estimates to the observation hydrograph.

symbols.visualObject: Radius of the uniform pumping circle
symbols.transition: Grow a radius from the observation well

return.visualObject: Original observation-well hydrograph centered in a bounded dense-pumping circle with recovered S and uncertain Q_e
return.claim: The equivalent Theis response can estimate representative storativity from observation-well drawdown without individual pumping-well information.
return.claimBoundary: Transmissivity must be known or constrained; records should be short and pumping dominated; dense uniform pumping is the intended setting; distance, arrangement, and anisotropy bias equivalent pumping more strongly than storativity.
return.sourceExcerpt: estimates the pumping rate and storativity with reliability.

visual.primaryObject: Observation-well drawdown time series in a confined aquifer
visual.accentObject: Finite circular domain of equivalent uniform pumping
visual.geometryNotes: Keep the observation well fixed at the circle center. Convert individual Theis kernels into weighted rings, show anisotropy by elliptical drawdown maps without changing the circular averaging assumption, and display errors as intervals or residual fields.
